episodes.title: Utah Has More MLMs Per Capita Than Any U.S. State || Mormon Culture Created the Perfect Conditions
episodes.description:
Utah has more MLMs per capita than any state in the country — at least 15 major multi-level marketing companies headquartered there, and the three biggest, Young Living, Doterra, and Nu Skin, were all founded by members of the LDS Church. This week on What Do You Know Wednesday, Jess walks Hannah through why that isn’t a coincidence.

The pitch lands differently when you were raised inside the system that built it. Ward lists become warm sales databases. Two years of door-to-door missionary work become a sales skill. Testimony culture trains you to treat “I tried this oil and my migraines went away” as proof. Add the stay-at-home-mom economy and a prosperity-gospel thread that reads financial success as spiritual worthiness, and you get a population primed to buy in.

We get into the numbers the companies publish themselves: 89% of active Young Living members averaging four dollars a year in commissions, 86% of Doterra advocates earning under a thousand, and the top one percent of earners taking home more than 70% of total payouts. We talk about Utah’s white-collar crime registry, Dallin Oaks diagnosing the problem decades before he ran the church that created the conditions for it, and the Nu Skin founder who left to serve as a mission president in France while his company paid the FTC millions over deceptive income claims.

LulaRoe, Stampin’ Up, Melaleuca, Whitney Rose’s Wild Rose Beauty — if you were raised in this, you already know the pitch. 

By the end, Hannah has a lot more questions. 
Sorry for what we said when we were Mormon.

episodes.title: Reckless Ben vs. Bricks & Minifigs: The Mormon Mafia Claim
episodes.description:
A missing Lego Star Wars collection somehow became one of the strangest Mormon-adjacent internet stories of the year so far.
Bryan Mansell says his family’s valuable Lego collection was taken after a Bricks & Minifigs consignment deal went bad. Reckless Ben turned the dispute into a viral investigation. Then American Fork police got involved. There were searches, arrests, charges, a lawsuit, a restraining order, a closed Oregon franchise, and a growing online claim that this was all really about the “Mormon mafia.”
In this Postmormon Postmortem mini episode, we separate what’s documented from what people want to be true.
There’s also no honest way to talk about this story without talking about Utah County, Mormon social networks, in-group protection, reputation management, and the way shared religious culture can shape business, policing, and public trust.
You don’t need a secret Mormon cabal to explain this. You need a tight community, an instinct to protect your own, and a police department operating inside a heavily Mormon social world.

episodes.title: LDS Church Historian Apologizes for Racist Remarks
episodes.description:
Elder Kyle S. McKay, a General Authority Seventy and Church Historian for The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, apologized after making racially offensive remarks at the Yukon Oklahoma Stake conference.

In this Mormon Monday, we talk about what McKay said about “This Little Light of Mine,” why the references to Black religious expression, slavery language, and “Swanee River” were troubling, and why his apology was unusually direct for a senior Mormon leader.

We also look at the larger issue: what this moment reveals about Mormon culture, race, authority, and the gap between official church statements and what still happens in lived church spaces.

episodes.title: You Didn't Get a Comment. You Got a Node. The LDS Influence Machine Explained.
episodes.description: Someone showed up in our comments with three arguments, one after another — missing scrolls, Chicago Fire, syncretism. Behind that comment is a funded nonprofit, studio space, and SEO training. Here's the machine. We traced a comment section exchange back to the Ancient America Foundation — a 501(c)(3) nonprofit whose stated mission pillars include "Missionary Productivity" and "Gospel Advocacy," and whose own website says it exists to do "things the institutional Church cannot do." Its creator arm, the Faith Creators Alliance, runs studios in Utah, coaches 70+ creators on SEO and audience growth, and feeds interested viewers directly to missionaries through a pipeline called Discover the Book of Mormon. The episode covers the Book of Abraham facsimile problem, why the missing scrolls argument fails on Joseph Smith's own paper trail, the two-layer irony of the Church's Gospel Topics Essay hedge being amplified at scale, and what Russell M. Nelson's 2018 social media fast looks like next to a 70-creator influence operation. You weren't imagining it. You were the only one who didn't know what you were dealing with. episodes.title: Joseph Smith’s Happiness Letter to Nancy Rigdon Was Worse Than We Thought
episodes.description:
Joseph Smith’s “Happiness Letter” is often quoted in LDS spaces for its opening line: “Happiness is the object and design of our existence.” But the full letter was connected to Nancy Rigdon, the 19-year-old daughter of Sidney Rigdon, after she refused Joseph Smith’s proposal of plural marriage. In this episode, we walk through the Nancy Rigdon account, John W. Rigdon’s later affidavit, the full text of the Happiness Letter, Joseph Smith’s later denial, Nancy’s confrontation, Sidney Rigdon’s private appeal, and Orson Hyde’s public attempt to discredit Nancy.

This is the story behind one of the most selectively quoted documents in Mormon history.

episodes.title: How Mormons Deliver Harm in the Language of Love
episodes.description:
Someone said something to you, and it sounded like love. They were calm. They may have said they were worried about you, or that they were praying for you, or that the ward was concerned. And you left the conversation feeling like you'd been searched — like someone had gone through your dresser drawers and left your garments spilling out — and you couldn't explain why, because nothing cruel had actually been said.

That's not a coincidence. It's a grammar.

This episode of Postmormon Postmortem — hosted by Jess and Hannah — names six specific practices used inside LDS culture and Mormon family life that deliver harm in the language of care. The result is always the same: the wound lands, and anyone who tries to name it gets handed the problem back.

What we cover:

Tone policing as spiritual authority — how the Mormon doctrinal framework (3 Nephi 11:29, James E. Faust's 1996 General Conference address) makes calm the evidence of righteousness and emotion the evidence of spiritual failure — so the person who causes harm automatically wins the moral argument by staying composed

Epistemic harm and the shift — how the conversation moves from what happened to how you responded to it, and why naming harm "with feeling attached" is treated as a spiritual violation inside Mormonism

The non-apology and how it functions — "I'm sorry you felt hurt," "the Church is perfect but the people aren't," and how institutional DARVO (Deny, Attack, Reverse Victim and Offender) pre-exonerates the system before anyone names a specific harm

Niceness vs. kindness in Mormon culture — what the Articles of Faith, Young Women's Values, and the Primary song "Kindness Begins With Me" actually teach, and how "love the sinner, hate the sin" lets someone perform warmth while holding a condemning belief

Concern trolling and the language of eternal stakes — how "we're worried about you" carries the weight of eternal families doctrine, why setting limits feels like breaking something sacred, and how genuine concern is distinguished from control disguised as care

Spiritual bypassing and "I'll pray for you" — psychologist John Welwood's concept of caring from a safe distance, and how collective ward fasting and temple prayer rolls let an entire community perform love while agreeing on the premise that you need fixing

Testimony as conversation ender — Boyd K. Packer's doctrine that a testimony is found in the bearing of it, how "I know the church is true" replaces engagement with certainty, and what it means to receive that certainty when what you needed was someone willing to not know

We also discuss Amira Barger's analysis of niceness as social control, Jennifer Freyd's institutional DARVO framework, research on non-apologies and how they often make people feel worse than no apology at all, and a 2013 Times and Seasons essay by LDS academics acknowledging that bearing testimony is "a conversation ender — sometimes that is what is called for."

Who this is for:

If you've ever left a conversation with a bishop, a parent, or a ward member feeling worse than when you walked in — and couldn't explain why, because everyone had been so kind — this episode is for you. If you grew up learning that calm meant righteous and upset meant out of alignment, and you're still unlearning it, this is for you. If someone you love told you they were praying for you and it felt like a door closing dressed as a door opening, this is for you. If you're still trying to find language for conversations that made you feel like the problem, here's some language.

The harm was real. The grammar was learned. And the words are yours now.
Related episodes: If this one hit, you'll want You Didn't Get a Comment, You Got a Node (on how LDS apologetics infrastructure operates in comment sections) and our continuing revelation episode, God Changed His Mind Again (on how doctrinal whiplash functions as institutional control).

episodes.title: The Mormon Church Still Hasn't Apologized For Mountain Meadows
episodes.description:
The Mountain Meadows Massacre is one of the darkest events in Mormon history. In 1857, Mormon settlers and local leaders murdered about 120 men, women, and children in southern Utah after promising them safe passage under a white flag.

More than 160 years later, the LDS Church still has not apologized.

In this minisode, Hannah asks one question: what would a real apology even sound like?

Using a ten-part framework for meaningful apologies, this episode walks through the apology the Church should have given, then compares it to what the institution actually did: blame the Paiutes, scapegoat John D. Lee, stay silent for generations, express “profound regret,” and clarify that “regret” did not mean “apology.”

What this covers:

The 1857 Mountain Meadows Massacre and the murder of about 120 emigrants in southern Utah;

What a real apology has to include: naming the offense, taking responsibility, admitting fault, explaining the cause, offering repair, and requesting forgiveness; 

How an honest LDS apology would have to address obedience, religious violence, blood atonement, outsider fear, and institutional self-protection;

John D. Lee, his conviction, execution, excommunication, and later restoration to full church membership;

Henry B. Eyring’s 2007 statement of “profound regret,” and the Church’s clarification that it was not an apology;

Gordon B. Hinckley’s 1999 Mountain Meadows memorial statement, including the explicit refusal to acknowledge Church complicity;

Dallin H. Oaks’s statement that the Church does not seek apologies and does not give them.

This episode is for anyone trying to understand what institutional accountability actually requires. It is for anyone who has been told that regret is enough, that time passing is repair, or that naming harm is the same as being divisive.

A real apology does more than sound sad. It tells the truth. It names what happened. It gives up the protection of ambiguity. It accepts the cost of repair.
The Mormon Church has never done that for Mountain Meadows.

episodes.title: What Is a Dry Mormon? Dirty Soda, Ballerina Farm, and the Second Mormon Moment
episodes.description:
New York magazine says America is in a second Mormon moment, and this time the church didn't do a thing — the influencers did it for them. This week on What Do You Know Wednesday, Jess puts Hannah on the spot with a phrase neither of us grew up with: the dry Mormon.
A dry Mormon lives the lifestyle without the theology. No alcohol, no coffee, clean eating, modest fashion, community-first values, and none of the belief. Some were never Mormon at all; they found the aesthetic on TikTok and stayed. We trace the term through dirty soda culture, the rise of influencers like Hannah Neeleman of Ballerina Farm and Taylor Frankie Paul of Secret Lives of Mormon Wives, and the Pew data showing devout traditionalists shrinking while cultural Mormons and the PIMO crowd grow. The church gains the wholesome branding while losing the believers, the tithing, and any control over the word it spent years trying to retire.
Find us at postmormonpostmortem.com and on the socials @postmormonpostmortem.

episodes.title: A Former Utah Supreme Court Justice Asked Dallin Oaks to Defend the Courts
episodes.description:
David Bennett, a former Utah Supreme Court justice, wrote to Dallin Oaks in the Salt Lake Tribune on May 14. Oaks is also a former Utah Supreme Court justice and the current president of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. The ask: speak out for Utah's courts.

The context is Proposition 4, the anti-gerrymandering law Utah voters passed in 2018. The legislature repealed it in 2020 and drew its own map. Five years of litigation followed. In late 2025 the courts threw out the legislature's replacement map and installed one drawn by plaintiffs including the League of Women Voters and Mormon Women for Ethical Government. Republican-appointed justices upheld those rulings, and a campaign to remove two of them followed.

The same month, the church directed every U.S. ward to hold a fifth Sunday lesson on religious freedom and constitutional principles, set for May 31, with a worldwide fast for the rule of law on July 5. 

Bennett's argument is that Oaks has the standing, the title, the legal background, and now the reach into every U.S. chapel to say that judges shouldn't lose their seats for ruling correctly.

Bennett's letter and additional sources are linked below.

episodes.title: The Mormon Church Admitted Joseph Smith's Polygamy, But Not the Coercion
episodes.description:
You were taught that polygamy was a hard thing, faithfully done. That God commanded it, that the men who lived it were obedient and the women who accepted it were worthy. That it ended in 1890. That we don’t do that anymore.

What we cover:
Fanny Alger — the teenage domestic worker in the Smith household, Joseph’s first plural wife in Kirtland;

William McLellin’s account that Emma found Joseph and Fanny together;

Oliver Cowdery’s 1838 letter calling it a “dirty, nasty, filthy affair”;

Zina Huntington Jacobs — six months pregnant by her husband Henry when she was sealed to Joseph in 1841, later sealed to Brigham Young too, with Henry shipped off on a mission to England;

Helen Mar Kimball — sealed to Joseph “several months before her fifteenth birthday,” told that her whole family’s salvation depended on her saying yes;

Heber C. Kimball — Helen’s own father and first counselor in the First Presidency — preaching from the pulpit that a man with one wife “begins to wither and dry up” and that being confined to one woman is “small business”;

The angel with the drawn sword — the threat-of-destruction Joseph told prospective wives;
Doctrine & Covenants 132:54 — addressed to Emma Smith, commanding her to accept or be destroyed;

The public denials — Joseph’s May 1844 sermon claiming one wife while married to more than thirty, the Times and Seasons certificate, the Nauvoo Expositor, and the press he ordered destroyed;
The Utah-era sermons calling monogamy a source of prostitution, disease, and the fall of Rome;

The 1890 Manifesto & the 1904 Manifesto — what Wilford Woodruff’s statement does and doesn’t say, the plural marriages apostles kept performing after 1890, and Joseph F. Smith’s testimony to the U.S. Senate;

The revisionist claim: the no-longer-fringe position that Joseph never practiced polygamy and Brigham Young fabricated D&C 132;

The General Handbook: a man can be sealed to multiple women, living or dead; a woman cannot — and the celestial polygamy still structuring what living members are promised in the eternities.

This episode is for you if you were taught polygamy ended in 1890 and “we don’t do that anymore,” without knowing that it’s still structuring what’s expected of you after death. And it’s for you if you’re a woman who was told you’ll accept plural marriage in the next life whether you like it or not, a descendant of plural wives, or someone who loves one — and you’ve been waiting for the institution to reckon with what the doctrine cost the women it names. This episode won’t do that either, but it will be honest about the fact that it hasn’t happened.

The church named the women and called Emma’s experience an excruciating ordeal. It never named the coercion, never used the word consent, and never removed the doctrine. Naming what happened and reckoning with it are different things, and the gap between them is where the real accounting still needs to happen.

episodes.title: The Mormon Church Disavowed 126 Years of Racist Doctrine. They Called It "Theories."
episodes.description:
You were taught that where you were born — and to whom — was God's assessment of what you'd done before you arrived on earth. That dark skin was the record of pre-mortal failure. That there was no injustice whatsoever in any of it. And if you grew up in the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, you weren't just taught this in passing. You were taught it from the pulpit, in Sunday school, in officially endorsed books sold at Deseret Book, and in signed statements from the First Presidency. For 126 years.

In 2013, the church published a Gospel Topics Essay on the priesthood and temple restrictions. Jess and Hannah of Postmormon Postmortem read it so you don't have to — and more importantly, they read what came before it, so you can hear exactly what the essay is and isn't saying.

What we cover:
- Brigham Young's 1852 speeches to the Utah Territorial Legislature, including the February address delivered the same day as the bill legalizing slavery in Utah Territory — and what he said the condition for lifting the ban actually was
- Elijah Abel — the black elder ordained to the Melchizedek priesthood in the 1840s, what happened to his priesthood line after 1852, and why the 2013 essay names him in its opening and stops there
- Mark E. Peterson's 1954 BYU address, "Race Problems as They Affect the Church," and the pre-mortal valiance framework that gave the ban its theological architecture
- Bruce R. McConkie's Mormon Doctrine — published by Deseret Book, on shelves in LDS homes for decades, cited in Sunday school — and what it says under the entry "Negroes"
- The 1949 First Presidency statement signed by George Albert Smith, J. Reuben Clark, and David O. McKay: "a direct commandment from the Lord," "no injustice whatsoever" — and why the 2013 essay doesn't cite it
- The 1969 First Presidency statement — same David O. McKay, 20 years later, same policy, with the certainty quietly drained out of it
- The 1978 revelation, Official Declaration 2, and what it does and doesn't say — including the Brazil temple problem that made the ban institutionally unenforceable
- The word "theories" — what it's doing, who it's covering for, and what the 1949 First Presidency statement actually called this doctrine
- Dallin H. Oaks describing, in his own words at the 2018 Be One commemoration, that he could not receive spiritual confirmation of the theological justifications — and what he did with that
- Why the essay is a disavowal and not an apology, and what the difference costs the people it doesn't name

This episode is for you if you were taught the pre-mortal valiance framework — in Sunday school, at home, from a parent who had the answers — and never had language for what that teaching actually did. It's for you if you've read the 2013 essay and felt like something important was missing but couldn't name what. And it's for you if you're a black Latter-day Saint, a descendant of black Latter-day Saints, or someone who loves one — and you've been waiting for the institution to name you specifically. This episode won't do that either, but it will be honest about the fact that it hasn't happened.

The church closed a practice without explaining it. Thirty-five years later, the essay attempted the reckoning that 1978 didn't make. Those are different things — and the gap between them is where the real accounting still needs to happen.

If this episode is part of how you're working through the church's history, you might also want Face In a Hat: What the Gospel Topics Essays Admit About Book of Mormon Translation and Joseph Smith Labeled Every Figure Wrong. The LDS Church Knows. Here's Their Essay. — both part of Postmormon Postmortem's ongoing Gospel Topics Essay series.

episodes.title: Mormon Baptisms for the Dead: Anne Frank, Hitler, and Holocaust Victims
episodes.description:
Welcome to the second episode of What Do You Know Wednesday! 

This week Jess asks Hannah, what do you know about baptisms for the dead?

Baptism for the dead is one of the most distinctive — and controversial — practices in the LDS Church. A living member stands in as physical proxy and gets baptized on behalf of someone who’s dead. Jess and Hannah both did it as teenagers. Standard youth activity.

What we didn’t know at the time: the church has performed proxy baptisms for George Washington, all signers of the Declaration of Independence, Adolf Hitler, the parents of Nazi hunter Simon Wiesenthal, Barack Obama’s mother, and approximately 650,000 Holocaust victims. Anne Frank is believed to have been baptized by proxy at least nine times, despite formal agreements with Jewish organizations dating back to 1992 to stop.

Daniel Pearl — the Wall Street Journal reporter kidnapped and killed in Pakistan in 2002 because he was Jewish — was baptized by proxy at the LDS Temple in Twin Falls, Idaho in 2011. NPR confirmed it. His widow called it a lack of respect for Danny and for his parents.

The church frames each case as an unauthorized individual acting against official policy. Critics note that the church runs one of the most sophisticated genealogical databases in the world.

episodes.title: The Same Playbook: How High-Control Religion, MLMs, and Cults Use Identical Tactics
episodes.description:
You've done the work on the church. You understand, intellectually, why it was built the way it was built. And then you walk into a room — a business meeting, a family dinner, a political rally — and you feel it again. That specific pressure. The one that says: agree, perform, belong. The one that makes doubt feel like a character flaw.

That feeling has a name. And it didn't originate in Salt Lake City.

This is the final episode of Postmormon Postmortem's four-part series on Mormon Machinery, hosted by Jess. Episodes one through three traced the specific mechanisms of the LDS Church. This one zooms out entirely — because the mechanisms don't stay there, and once you can see them clearly in one place, you start seeing them everywhere.

What we cover:
- Robert Lifton's 8 features of thought reform environments (1961) — milieu control, mystical manipulation, demand for purity, confession, sacred science, loading the language, doctrine over person, and dispensing of existence — and how to use them as a diagnostic framework, not a checklist

- Stephen Hassan's BITE Model — behavior control, information control, thought control, and emotional control — and why the more useful question is never "is this a cult?" but "how many of these features are present, and how intensively?"

- The Utah MLM pipeline — why Utah has more multi-level marketing companies per capita than any other state, why major MLMs  are headquartered there, and why researchers, sociologists, and industry analysts attribute this directly to LDS cultural structures

- Evangelical megachurches and the prosperity gospel — how the same mechanisms run in different buildings with different vocabulary, and why the scandals coming out of those institutions look identical to what you already know

- High-control political movements — how the BITE model features appear across the political spectrum, what "loading the language" looks like in political movements versus religious ones, and why the thought-terminating clichés function identically regardless of ideology

 -The LDS Church and family systems as overlapping institutions — how the church provided divine sanction for patriarchal authority in the home, using the David Archuleta and Jeff Archuleta case as a detailed example of priesthood authority functioning as cover for family control

- Marlene Winell's religious trauma syndrome (coined 2011) — why the symptoms overlap significantly with PTSD, why the recovery literature for high-control religion and high-control family systems is largely the same, and why the therapists who specialize in one tend to specialize in the other

- Diagnostic questions for identifying high-control environments — does the group have a mechanism for being wrong? Is doubt treated as information? Would expressing it publicly cost you something? Is belonging contingent on performance? — and why the questions feeling dangerous is itself data
What survivors of high-control environments carry out with them — specifically, the unusually developed capacity to question authority, read institutional behavior, and identify the gap between what a group says and what it does

This episode is for you if you left Mormonism and keep recognizing the same dynamics somewhere else — in a job, a relationship, a political movement, a family structure — and you can't quite articulate why it feels so familiar. It's for you if you've never been Mormon but someone in your life has, and this is the episode they wanted you to hear. It's for you if you've been doing your own deconstruction work in any tradition — evangelical, high-demand, politically high-control — and you needed someone to hand you the vocabulary for what you lived inside. It's for you if you've been trying to figure out why leaving the church felt like leaving a family, or why your family felt like a church. Jess has a specific answer for that, and it lands.

The critical thinking you had to build to see any of this clearly — that came from having to think your way out of something that was designed not to be thought about. That is not nothing. You paid for it. But you have it.

If this is your entry point to the series, start with Episode 1: The Mormon Machinery.

episodes.title: Mormon Sibling Violence Has a Pattern. We Have 100 Years of Headlines.
episodes.description:
On June 18, 1964, a 15-year-old Mormon boy in Basalt, Colorado gave his younger brother a command. His brother ignored it. He fired a warning shot at his feet. His brother turned around and said: I told you I am invulnerable just like Superman. There is really nothing you can do to me. Stephen Ferrin fired twice more. David Ferrin, 13 years old, died. The obituary described him as the victim of a Thursday shooting. It did not say who killed him.

On November 7, 1993, Joe DiLello spent four hours on a couch in Clearfield, Utah, waiting for his brother Michael to kick through the front door. He had already read Utah's home defense statute. When Michael arrived, Joe fired 14 times. Ten shots connected. Michael DiLello's obituary in the Deseret News said he passed away November 7, 1993. It did not say how. It did not say who.

Two brothers killed by their brothers in Mormon communities, 30 years apart. This episode documents the theological architecture underneath both cases — the Nephi narrative, priesthood worthiness as public family grading, patriarchal blessings, exaltation theology, and eternal sealing — and traces a pattern of fraternal violence and institutional erasure across 100 years of Mormon community headlines.

Trigger warnings: homicide, family violence, death of a child.

episodes.title: Mormon Monday: The LDS Church’s Constitution Lesson
episodes.description:
The Church doesn’t usually write the fifth Sunday lesson. That’s left to local leaders who know what the congregation needs. But in March, the First Presidency wrote this lesson themselves. On May 31st, it arrives in every American meeting house simultaneously.

This is Mormon Monday, a weekly short-form segment from Postmormon Postmortem where Hannah and Jess break down one thing about Mormonism that caught their attention this week. This week: a coordinated fifth Sunday lesson on the U.S. Constitution and what it signals about where the institutional church is headed.

What we cover this week:

The First Presidency letter directing all U.S. wards and branches to teach the same lesson on May 31st. This lesson teaches that the Constitution and Declaration of Independence enabled the restoration of the church.

Dallin Oaks’ entire apostolic career as a religious freedom litigator — amicus briefs in Masterpiece Cakeshop, Fulton v. Philadelphia, and 303 Creative, all three turning on whether organizations can claim exemptions from LGBTQ nondiscrimination law. He is now president of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. His legal project just became a Sunday lesson.

The July 5th worldwide fast: a U.S.-only curriculum, but all 17.5 million members in 170 countries asked to fast for American religious liberty.

Why that asymmetry makes sense inside Mormon theology — America as the promised land, the Constitution as divinely inspired — and why it lands differently when you grew up outside the United States.

The discussion guide’s language that “too many of God’s children have been and still are oppressed” — and what referent that phrase finds in a room that’s been hearing about Missouri and Nauvoo since Primary.

Mountain Meadows (1857): 120 immigrants massacred by Mormon settlers in Utah; why it’s a footnote when the inverse would be doctrine, monument, and annual observance.

Hans Mattson — former Area Authority Seventy from Sweden — describing a training meeting where Gordon B. Hinckley told leaders that missionary efforts would concentrate on North America because Europe was dying, Africa was a drain, and North America was where the tithing money was.

If you grew up being taught that America is the promised land and the Constitution was divinely inspired for the restoration — and never stopped to question why an allegedly global institution had a specifically American theological origin story – hey, join the club! And if you grew up Mormon outside the United States, this one is especially for you. 

We’re watching the church coordinate political messaging through Sunday curriculum while calling it doctrine. The money will tell you where the priorities are.

New to Postmormon Postmortem? Welcome! We’re glad you’re here.

episodes.title: Book of Abraham: Reading the Evidence the LDS Church Can't Explain Away
episodes.description:
You were taught that the Book of Abraham was translated by a prophet from ancient Egyptian papyri — physical documents, in his hands, word for word from Abraham himself. That's not a fringe interpretation. That's the official language used in church materials for 180 years. And the papyri survived. Egyptologists read them. And the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints published an essay in 2014 acknowledging what they found.

This episode of Postmormon Postmortem with Jess and Hannah is a close read of that essay — not just for what it admits, but for how it's written, because the how matters as much as the what.

What we cover:

- The 1835 purchase of Egyptian mummies and papyri in Kirtland, Ohio — what Joseph Smith claimed to have found and what the church's own essay says about it

- Why the Book of Abraham is load-bearing LDS theology — the pre-existence, the plurality of gods, the council in heaven, the doctrine of human deification — and why the church cannot quietly set it aside

- The 1912 Franklin Spalding analysis: an Episcopal bishop sent the three facsimiles to eight of the world's leading Egyptologists and Semitists independently. All eight reached the same conclusion. The results were published in the New York Times. The church had access that day.

- The First Presidency's letter: written before any Egyptological analysis, stating the fragments "support completely" Joseph Smith's claims
What the Egyptologists actually found: a standard funerary text called the Book of Breathings, prepared for a man named Hor — whose name is on the document. Nothing in it mentions Abraham.

- Robert Ritner, the Roe Professor of Egyptology at the Oriental Institute of the University of Chicago — one of the foremost Egyptologists of his generation — and his formal 2014 response to the essay. His conclusion: the Book of Abraham is "a perhaps well-meaning, but erroneous invention by Joseph Smith." He died in 2021. The essay remains on the website unchanged.

T- he three facsimiles and Joseph Smith's published labels for every figure — and what those figures actually are. Osiris on a funerary lion bed. Anubis with a chiseled-off snout. The creator god Min-Ammon depicted in an act of creation. The four sons of Horus. Joseph got every single one wrong.

 - The Kirtland Papers — the Egyptian Alphabet and Grammar documents Joseph and his scribes produced in 1835, held in the church vault, found by BYU scholars in 1933, left there, and not made public until Gerald and Sandra Tanner published them in 1966. They show Joseph actively building a system for decoding Egyptian — which is a problem for the church's current explanation of how the translation happened.

- The three explanations the Gospel Topics Essay offers — the Missing Scroll theory, the Catalyst theory, and the redefinition of translation — and why the essay presents all three without endorsing any of them

- Why the Catalyst theory requires a God who deceives his own prophet for no apparent reason — and what the grammar documents do to that argument

- The informed consent question: if members had known this from the beginning, would they have committed the way they did — tithing, callings, community, family structure, everything?

This episode is for you if you were raised on the idea that Joseph Smith translated ancient documents and you've never been given a clean account of what happened when scholars checked that claim. It's for you if you're in a faith crisis and you keep being told to just pray about it. It's for you if someone you love is defending something that the church's own essay quietly admits it cannot defend.

The evidence here isn't ambiguous. The only question is what you do with it — and that part is yours.

Related: Episode 1 of our Gospel Topics Essay series covers the Book of Mormon translation essay — the seer stone, the hat, and what the church now admits about how it happened.

episodes.title: The Ensign Peak Billions: What Your Tithing Actually Built
episodes.description:
You paid a full ten percent. Every year. Before taxes if you were doing it the way the church prefers. You sat in worthiness interviews and confirmed it. You bore testimony about it in sacrament meeting. You were told the windows of heaven were tied to that number.

The church took that money, grew it quietly inside a fund most members had never heard of, and built $265 billion in total assets over decades — while hiding the investment portfolio behind thirteen shell companies specifically designed to keep members from finding out how much was there. The SEC noticed in 2023. The fine was five million dollars. The church called the matter closed.

This episode of Postmormon Postmortem is Jess working through one question from the ground up: what does it actually cost to belong to the LDS Church, and where does that money go?

What we cover:

The Ensign Peak portfolio — founded 1997, $32 billion by 2018, hidden from federal regulators for twenty-two years through shell companies whose managers were chosen for their common names and limited social media presence so they couldn't be traced back to the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.

The SEC cease-and-desist order — what the federal filing actually says, why a former senior SEC counsel said a private institution would have faced a much larger penalty, and what "institutional deference" means in practice.

Lifetime tithing math — median US household income, gross vs. net, forty years of active membership, and what the cumulative number actually is before you finish reading this sentence.

The temple recommend system as financial compliance mechanism — how the worthiness interview ties full spiritual participation to verified tithing payment, and what it means to be locked out of your own child's wedding ceremony because of a financial shortfall.

Costly signaling — the psychological research on why sacrifice increases group attachment, and how every demand the church makes (tithing, missions, callings, temple attendance) functions to raise the psychological cost of leaving.

The unpaid labor economy — callings, the bishop's actual job description, seventy thousand missionaries paying to serve as the church's uncompensated sales force, and the FamilySearch genealogical database built on volunteer labor driven by theological mandate.

The social architecture of exit — Word of Wisdom compliance as social separation, mission service as peer cohort disruption, temple worthiness as a visible hierarchy that puts you outside the building at your sibling's wedding.

The Numbers beat — $265 billion total assets, $182 billion in investments, $83 billion in real estate and operating assets, $56.8 billion in the publicly reportable Ensign Peak stock portfolio as of late 2024, annual investment income that now exceeds annual tithing revenue, and $2.5 billion in humanitarian giving claimed over thirty-six years — distributed in a single year as investment returns.

The distinction that matters — the institution is cynical; the members mostly aren't; and why that distinction is not a small thing

This episode is for you if you paid your tithing faithfully and never thought to ask where it went. If you sat in a tithing settlement and something in the room felt off but you didn't have language for it yet. If you stood outside a temple building while your family went in and were told that was a you problem. If you are a currently active member and your discomfort right now is real and you want to know what to do with it. If you left the church and you have been trying to explain to someone who wasn't raised in it why leaving cost you everything — your relationships, your professional network, your neighborhood, your family's trust. If you are that person trying to understand why someone you love is as wrecked as they are.

Your discomfort isn't a spiritual problem. It's information.

episodes.title: The Mormon Machine: $265 Billion, Hidden Wealth, and How It Works
episodes.description:
The Mormon Church currently holds an estimated $265 billion in assets. Its investment income now exceeds what it collects from member tithing. And it still requires 10% of your income for life to remain a full member.

This episode is the bird's eye view — the structural overview that explains how the LDS Church was built, how it sustains itself, and why it keeps working even when members find out things like this.

THE FOUNDING: Before Joseph Smith was a prophet, he was a treasure seeker. Literally charging people money to locate buried treasure using a special rock. In 1827, he claimed an angel delivered golden plates containing new scripture. The translation happened with that same seer stone placed in a hat, face-down, in the dark. The golden plates weren't even in the room for most of it. This is in the church's own Gospel Topics Essays — published in 2013, quietly, and never taught in Sunday school.

FINANCIAL EXTRACTION: Tithing isn't voluntary in any functional sense. 10% of your income for life is the price of a temple recommend — and without one, you cannot attend your own child's wedding in the church's most sacred buildings. The church does not publish its finances. In 2023, the SEC fined the church and its investment arm, Ensign Peak Advisors, $5 million for using 13 shell companies to hide a $32 billion stock portfolio from federal regulators for 20 years — with full knowledge and approval of senior leadership.

THE MISSION: 18 to 19 year olds, before their brains are fully developed, are sent out for two years (18 months if you're a woman). Paid for by the missionary and their family. Two by two, always monitored, no social media, no unsupervised outside contact. Their job: door-to-door sales for the institution that shaped them since birth. Over 100,000 missionaries deployed worldwide. Every one of them paid for the privilege. The mission is not just recruitment — it's a mechanism for manufacturing personal investment so deep that walking away means confronting whether you gave two years of your life to something that wasn't real.

TEMPLE SECRECY: The most sacred ceremonies happen behind closed doors, and members take oaths not to disclose what happens there. Historically, those oaths included pantomimed penalties — gestures imitating having your throat cut, your body dismembered. Quietly removed in 1990 when they became publicly known. No explanation given. Secret information is also, not coincidentally, information that is very hard to think critically about. You can't fact-check something you're not supposed to discuss.

THE REVELATION MACHINE: The church ended polygamy in 1890 — the year federal seizure of church property was becoming a real problem. It ended the Black priesthood ban in 1978 — the year it was aggressively expanding into Brazil and West Africa, where a whites-only priesthood was a logistical obstacle. It introduced the 2015 exclusion policy barring children of same-sex couples from baptism, called it revelation, watched LGBTQ suicides spike, and reversed it 41 months later. Also called revelation. No apology either time. Revelation is remarkably sensitive to institutional pressure.

GENEALOGY: The LDS Church's FamilySearch database contains over 20 billion records. It was built almost entirely by volunteers who believed they were doing sacred work for their dead ancestors. By 2014 it was 32 times the size of the Library of Congress, and it adds the equivalent of another Library of Congress every single year. The institution owns the database, controls access to certain records, and holds licensing partnerships including with Ancestry.com. The members believed they were engaged in eternal salvation. The institution got infrastructure worth hundreds of millions of dollars. That's not individual intent. That's structural outcome.

Once you see the machinery, you'll see it everywhere — in MLMs, in authoritarian political movements, in abusive family systems. The LDS Church just built an unusually clean, unusually well-funded, unusually well-documented version of it.

episodes.title: Face in a Hat: What the Gospel Topics Essays Admit About Book of Mormon Translation
episodes.description:
There's a painting — Joseph Smith at a table, golden plates open, fingers on the characters. It hung on the wall of your Sunday school classroom. It ran in the Ensign for decades. Nobody told you that every person actually in that room — Emma Smith, Oliver Cowdery, Martin Harris, David Whitmer — described something different. A small brown river rock. A hat that Joseph pressed his face into. The plates, covered with a cloth. Sometimes not even in the room. The church had the correct information the whole time.

This is Episode 1 of our eight-part series on the Gospel Topics Essays: what the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints officially admits about its own history, and what those admissions cost.

This episode covers:
The Gospel Topics Essays: published on the church's website in 2013 with no announcement, no First Presidency letter, and no introduction to local leaders — buried six to seven clicks deep in their sitemap

The 150-year visual record: BYU professor Anthony Sweat found 17 images of Joseph translating used across 55 Ensign appearances. Every one showed open plates. Not one showed a hat.

The seer stone's institutional history: documented openly through the 1920s, called "all hearsay" by Joseph Fielding Smith in 1956, disappeared from lesson manuals, and quietly reappeared in the 2013 essay

The catalyst theory: the church's theological response to the translation problem — if the plates were just a trigger and the words came by revelation, the Book of Mormon's claim to being a translation of a specific ancient record becomes unverifiable

Jeremy Runnells, brought before a disciplinary council for apostasy in 2014 for publicly discussing information the church had published on its own website that same year

Elder Stephen Snow's framing of the essay rollout as inoculation — managed exposure — not transparency

If you bore testimony of the painting version in sacrament meeting or across a table on your mission, this episode is for you. If you've just found the Gospel Topics Essays and want to understand what the church is actually saying versus carefully not saying, Jess and Hannah lay it out plainly — no apologetics, no rage, just the record against the framing.

The church didn't just hide a method. It let you build a testimony on your understanding of a word — translation — and then quietly changed what the word means. You weren't wrong to believe what you were taught. You were taught the version the institution decided you were ready for.

episodes.title: Mormon Stories Lawsuit — The Trademark the LDS Church Called a Victory for Satan
episodes.description:
You built a marketing campaign called "I'm a Mormon." You put it on billboards in Times Square. You ran it for eight years. Then your new president stood at a pulpit and told your members that every use of that word was a major victory for Satan. You scrubbed the website. You renamed the choir. You rewrote the style guide. Then you filed a federal lawsuit to protect your right to it.

We break down the LDS Church's April 2026 federal lawsuit against Mormon Stories and its host, John Dehlin — and why the story is stranger than any headline made it sound.

What we cover:

The "I'm a Mormon" campaign (2010–2018): what it cost, what it was designed to do, and why the church's own research found the top five public descriptors of "Mormon" were cultish, secretive, sexist, anti-gay, and controlling

Russell Nelson's 2018 General Conference address declaring the word Mormon a "major victory for Satan" — and the institutional whiplash that followed for members, employees, and the Mormon Tabernacle Choir

Trademark abandonment as a legal doctrine: how sustained non-use or behavior inconsistent with ownership can be used to challenge a trademark 
registration — and why the church's eight-year campaign against the word is now their biggest legal liability

The USPTO's 2005 rejection of the church's broad trademark application on "Mormon" — and why you can't trademark Lutheran or Methodist either

The church's narrow existing trademark registrations (genealogy services, educational conferences) and the 2026–2027 renewal window that requires proof of active commercial use — proof that is now very hard to produce

Why filing a high-profile federal lawsuit eight months before the trademark renewal window opens is one way to generate documented enforcement evidence

John Dehlin's 21-year run under the name Mormon Stories, his 2015 public excommunication for apostasy, and why his lawyers will argue the excommunication itself functions as a disclaimer

The November 2025 IP letter, the logo change from navy to orange, the written disclaimers added across YouTube, Spotify, Facebook, and the website — and why the church said it wasn't enough

The mediation breakdown: the church's demand for an audio disclaimer every 15 minutes throughout each episode, and Dehlin's argument that renaming it "Ex-Mormon Stories" would be factually inaccurate since the show regularly features believing members

The consumer confusion argument: screenshots of YouTube and Facebook comments from listeners who initially thought Mormon Stories was affiliated with the church and why the show's homepage categories make that confusion very hard to sustain

The copyright claims (church-owned photographs used in thumbnails) versus the trademark claims —  why these are legally very different arguments

What a federal judge in Utah is actually being asked to decide

This is for you if you've been watching the Mormon Stories lawsuit news and felt like something important was missing from the coverage. It's for you if you know who John Dehlin is and you want the legal and institutional context, not just the headline. It's for you if you left the church and you remember being told to proudly say "I'm a Mormon" — and you've been sitting with the cognitive dissonance of watching the institution now sue to control the word they told you to own.

The church told members every use of the word was a gift to the adversary. Then they filed in federal court to prove it still belongs to them. That's not just irony. That's a $200 billion institution that will not let go of a name it publicly abandoned — because letting go means losing the legal right to control who gets to use it.

If you want to go deeper on how the church responds to institutional dissent, our episode "Why They Can't Hear You" covers the three mechanisms the church uses to make criticism structurally impossible.

episodes.title: Leaving Mormonism – Why People Leave, What Happens to Families, and What Life Actually Looks Like on the Other Side
episodes.description:
This is the final episode in the Postmormon Postmortem Foundations series. Over nine episodes, we've covered Mormon beliefs, temple culture, worthiness interviews, patriarchal blessings, mission culture, folklore, dietary laws, gender roles, and control mechanisms. This episode covers what happens when it all becomes too much — and why leaving is both harder and more liberating than most people expect.

WHY PEOPLE LEAVE: A faith crisis in Mormonism is rarely a sudden decision. It's typically a gradual process of discovering information that contradicts core beliefs — often information the church knew and did not share. Common triggers: learning the full scope of Joseph Smith's polygamy (including teenagers, and wives of living men); the DNA evidence against the Book of Mormon's central claims; the Book of Abraham papyri being identified as a standard Egyptian funeral text; the November 2015 LGBTQ policy and its 41-month reversal; the church's treatment of abuse survivors; the $150 billion investment portfolio; and the ongoing pattern of revelation that tracks political and social pressure.The church frames all of this as your failing. If you leave because of racism, you're too political. If you leave because of LGBTQ issues, you don't understand God's plan. If you leave because of abuse cover-ups, you're letting Satan win. The system is engineered so that the moment you start asking questions, you already feel like you're doing something morally wrong.

DR. MARLENE WINNELL'S FIVE PHASES: Hannah traces the recovery framework from Leaving the Fold, Dr. Winnell's essential book on breaking away from high-control fundamentalist religion. The phases: Separation (grief, guilt, depression, altered sense of self); Confusion (torn between two worlds, no new framework yet built); Avoidance (distancing from all things religious, throwing out the baby with the bathwater); Intense Mixed Feelings (unexpectedly flooded by emotion — Hannah describes this happening to her in Nauvoo, Illinois, watching missionary actors in pioneer costumes tell faith-promoting stories while her parents beamed); and Rebuilding (ongoing, nonlinear, always beginning before the previous phases are complete).

WHAT LEAVING COSTS: In Mormon culture, you don't just lose a religion. You lose your community, your identity, your social network, your professional relationships (in Utah, your bishop may be your boss), your family relationships, your eternal family narrative, your entire cosmology. The Mormon universe is extraordinarily specific — pre-mortal existence, spirit siblings, three degrees of glory, eternal progression toward Godhood, planetary rulership. Leaving isn't like leaving generic Christianity. You're losing an entire mapped universe with charts and diagrams and manuals.

FAMILIES AND CUSTODY: The church teaches — explicitly and implicitly — that believing spouses are justified in divorcing apostates, because eternal families require both partners to be faithful. Custody agreements in heavily Mormon areas sometimes specify church attendance requirements, restrict apostates from discussing their reasons for leaving, and reduce visitation if the non-believing parent doesn't maintain word of wisdom compliance. The eternal family doctrine becomes a hostage mechanism.

FOWLER'S STAGES: Hannah traces the conjunctive faith stage — where ambiguity becomes tolerable, certainty gives way to humility, and the collapse of one worldview creates space for a broader, more complex one. The gain embedded in the loss: I no longer have all the answers I thought I had, and that's okay.

THE EX-MORMON COMMUNITY: The internet broke the church's information monopoly. ExMormon.org, Reddit's r/exmormon, TikTok, and podcasts like this one have created communities where people find out — often for the first time — that what happened to them happened to thousands of others. Hannah stumbled into a private Facebook group for UK former Mormons and found family members she didn't know had left.

THE OTHER SIDE: Jess has been out for over 30 years. Hannah for decades. Both report that life on the other side is more authentic, more joyful, and more fully themselves than anything before. You get your Sundays back. You get 10% of your income back. And eventually, after the therapy and the time and the work, you get to ask "what do I want?" instead of "what's the right thing?" For many, that question is revolutionary.

episodes.title: David Archuleta Did Everything the LDS Church Asked. It Almost Killed Him.
episodes.description:
You learned early that your instincts were the least trustworthy thing about you. That the man above you — your father, your bishop, your mission president — had a line to God that you didn't, and that your job was to defer to it. Not because you were weak. Because that was called righteousness. Because the whole system was built to make compliance feel like virtue and resistance feel like sin.

In this episode of Postmormon Postmortem, Jess and Hannah dig into David Archuleta's memoir Devout: Losing My Faith to Find Myself — not as a celebrity story, but as one of the cleanest, most well-documented case studies we have of what the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints actually produces when its theology of priesthood authority, LGBTQ policy, and worthiness doctrine collides with a real human life over thirty years.

What we cover:
Scrupulosity — the clinically recognized religious subtype of OCD that David describes in Devout, and why a high-control religious environment doesn't just trigger it, it rewards it. His symptoms were indistinguishable from faithfulness. Missionaries called him quadrado — square — for being too obedient. He took it as a compliment. That's not incidental. That's the whole thing.

Jeff Archuleta and the priesthood — David's father wasn't just a controlling parent. He was David's priesthood holder. D&C 121 gives the church a built-in response to abusive priestly authority — unrighteous dominion — while providing zero mechanism for reporting it. The only people positioned to check priesthood power are the people who hold it.

The confession scene in Chile — David comes out to his mission president having written it down because he couldn't say it aloud. The response: you're a good missionary, keep going. He walks out still believing a solution exists. The loop restarts.

Three engagements — each entered in good faith inside a framework that told him marriage to a woman would make his attraction manageable. Each involving a real woman who deserved more honesty than the framework allowed either of them.

The November Policy — in 2015, the Church quietly updates its handbook. Same-sex married couples are apostates. Their children are barred from baptism and missionary service unless they disavow their parent's marriage at 18. Russell Nelson calls it revelation. Crisis hotlines report spikes in contacts from queer Mormon youth. There are documented suicides. The church acknowledges no connection.

Forty-one months later — the policy reverses. Also called revelation. No apology. No accounting. Just an updated handbook.

Russell Ballard's admission — that leadership had prayed about LGBTQ issues and received no clear answer, and that his conversation with David was the most in-depth he'd ever had with a gay person. An apostle. Setting policy. Having never asked.

Nashville, the pandemic, and what broke — after every loop the church handed him, David reaches a point where dying feels more honest than existing as a gay man inside a cosmology that has made those two things equivalent. Then something shifts. He describes feeling like God told him to stop asking to be changed.

What the church did after all of it — updated the handbook, called it revelation, and let David absorb the cost of a reckoning it still refuses to have.

This episode is for you if a priesthood holder shaped your childhood and you spent years calling your own resistance faithlessness. It's for you if you're LGBTQ+ and post-Mormon and still running the old loop. It's for you if someone you love is gay and still in the church and you can't understand why leaving feels like dying. The compliance you were trained into was not a character flaw. It was a feature. Naming that isn't apostasy. It's accuracy.

episodes.title: Why Mormon "Revelation" Arrives Right On Time
episodes.description:
When the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints shortened sacrament meeting for Palm Sunday, they called it inspired. When they reversed the November Policy in 2019 — classifying same-sex married couples as apostates and barring their children from baptism — that reversal was also revelation. When the priesthood and temple ban on Black members ended in 1978, that too was revelation. The timing is never a coincidence. And once you see the mechanism, you cannot unsee it.

In this episode of Post Mormon Postmortem, Jess and Hannah break down exactly how institutional policy becomes divine revelation — step by step, with primary sources, real comment section responses, and a live demonstration of DARVO playing out in their own mentions.

WHAT WE COVER:

THE MECHANISM IN FIVE STEPS: Identify a problem (burnout, declining attendance, legal pressure, cultural shift). Trial a solution quietly — commenter Betsy remembered as a child that her family had trialed the three-hour block before it was announced as revelation. Announce it as revelation. Frame any change as the Lord's timing. Members defend it as sacred — until the next change, when the cycle restarts.

THE PALM SUNDAY ANNOUNCEMENT: The Church announced on March 29th, 2026 that local congregations would hold a one-hour sacrament meeting instead of the usual two. Cited directly from the Church's own newsroom. This became Post Mormon Postmortem's highest-viewed TikTok ever — and what followed in the comments is a live case study in everything this episode covers.

THE LGBTQ POLICY TIMELINE: November 2015 — the November Policy barred children of same-sex couples from baptism and classified same-sex married couples as apostates. Context: Prop 8 backlash still fresh, the Supreme Court had just legalized same-sex marriage, the church was hemorrhaging members. April 2019 — complete reversal. Also revelation. In 41 months, people resigned their memberships, families were torn apart, and some people died by suicide. The church did not apologize. It moved on.

THE 2026 TRANSGENDER HANDBOOK UPDATE: Temple ordinances for transgender members must align with sex assigned at birth. Transgender members who pursue transition cannot work with children or youth. Their membership records are marked with the same designation used for people who have committed sexual abuse. This landed against a backdrop of 701 anti-trans bills in 44 US states in 2024 and legislation already being considered in 42 states in the first quarter of 2026.

THE 1978 PRIESTHOOD BAN REVERSAL: The temple in São Paulo, BYU athletic boycotts, documented institutional pressure — and then revelation. Jess's father came home from the temple and described the Black hands that came through the veil. He was shocked. Not angry — shocked. Because the church had spent his entire life teaching him that Black people bore the curse of Cain. The shock is the receipt.

DARVO EXPLAINED: Deny, Attack, Reverse Victim and Offender — coined by Dr. Jennifer Freyd. The Palm Sunday comment section delivered a textbook demonstration before breakfast. "Don't say untrue things." "Move on." "I'm talking about your pessimism." Jess cited the church's own website. She became the problem.

WHY MALE DEFENDERS SHOW UP THE WAY THEY DO: The priesthood is held by men. Institutional power is held by men. The first response when that system is examined comes from the people with the most invested in it. When you're raised to defer to priesthood authority, it is particularly galling when women say — out loud, with receipts — that they don't believe you.

WHO THIS EPISODE IS FOR: If you've ever noticed the cognitive dissonance when a policy reverses and no one apologizes — when the prophet seems to agree with whoever is in power in Washington — and been told that dissonance means your faith is weak: this episode is for you. That dissonance is not a personal failing. It is your brain doing exactly what it is supposed to do.If the schedule, the garments, the racial policy, the LGBTQ policy, and Palm Sunday recognition all changed — what exactly was revealed in the first place? Your brain already knows the answer.

episodes.title: Your Body Kept the Score: Somatic Healing After Leaving
episodes.description:
You've left the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints intellectually. You've read the history, examined the claims, worked through the theology. Your mind has moved on. So why does your body still react with fear when you disappoint an authority figure? Why does guilt show up when you do something that used to be forbidden? Why does your heart race at the thought of conflict or confrontation?

This episode answers that question — and tells you what actually helps.

WHY THE BODY STAYS STUCK: As Bessel van der Kolk wrote in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma is not just a story about something that happened. The bodies of trauma survivors continue to respond as if the danger is still present. Mormon theology specifically trained children's nervous systems in ways that produced lasting physiological dysregulation: omniscient surveillance ("Heavenly Father is always watching — including your thoughts"), eternal stakes ("your soul is on trial forever"), and attachment wounds (the people responsible for your safety transmitted the threat).

THE PSOAS AND THE BODY'S ALARM SYSTEM: The psoas muscle — the only muscle connecting your upper and lower body — is your fight-or-flight muscle. In chronic freeze or threat response, it maintains a state of microscopic contraction. This creates a feedback loop: your mind says you're safe in your living room in 2026, but your psoas is still signaling danger. This is why insight alone rarely completes healing.

FOUR APPROACHES THAT WORK AT THE BODY LEVEL: 
1. EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing): Francine Shapiro developed the protocol in 1987. Jess came to EMDR through a mountain biking accident — and what it did for that contained trauma opened the door to processing deeper material. WHO and the APA both recommend it. Van der Kolk described being initially skeptical before watching patients resolve years of stuck material in one or two sessions.

2. TRE (Trauma and Tension Releasing Exercises): David Berceli developed this after observing that people under extreme stress contract their bodies — and asking whether there was a genetically encoded release mechanism too. Jess found TRE on her living room floor out of desperation, after her physical therapist handed her a stapled packet of instructions. The tremoring that followed was, in her words, "nothing short of remarkable."

3. BREATHWORK: The only part of the autonomic nervous system that is both voluntary and involuntary. Rhythmic breathwork engages the parasympathetic system, signals the vagus nerve that the threat is gone, and can shift you into a fundamentally different physiological state. Hannah describes 90-minute breathwork sessions as among the most profoundly healing experiences of her life.

4. NEUROFEEDBACK: Sensors on the scalp read your brainwaves in real time and condition them toward calmer, more regulated states through classical conditioning. Hannah describes the first time she felt genuinely safe inside her own head — something she had never experienced before — as a result of neurofeedback.

WHO THIS IS FOR: Anyone who has done the intellectual work of leaving Mormonism or another high-control religion and still finds their body reacting as if the threat is present.

THIS IS EPISODE 4 OF OUR GREAT DIVIDE SERIES.

episodes.title: Healing After Leaving Mormonism — Identity, Grief, Self-Trust, Relationships, and Joy
episodes.description:
At 17 years old, Jess sat in the back row of sacrament meeting — in a mock turtleneck that hid bruises on her neck from the night before — and did the math. She had been sexually assaulted. She knew what Mormon purity culture said about what she was now. She knew where the blame would land. And sitting there watching her father on the stand with the bishopric, she decided: if this God would hold me accountable for what just happened to me, I don't believe in him. She knew she would have to leave. She was 17, alone in a room full of people who loved her, and she couldn't tell a single one of them the truth.

This episode is about what happens after you finally leave. It's the episode Jess and Hannah wish someone had handed them on the way out the door.

IDENTITY: The Mormon church doesn't just ask you to believe things. It tells you who you are — from before you can read. Daughter of Heavenly Father. Future wife and mother in the covenant. When you leave, you don't swap one belief system for another. You lose the entire framework through which you understood yourself. The work that follows isn't recovery of a self. There was no pre-Mormon self. It's discovery, often for the first time.

GRIEF: What post-Mormons experience isn't ordinary grief. It is the collapse of an entire existential architecture — where you came from, who you are, what you're doing here, where you're going, what every relationship means. One former member described his faith like a rock wall that filled his entire vision and supported his weight. When it collapsed, he felt himself suspended in nothingness, the floor gone. Ordinary grief frameworks don't fit because the tools you'd use to process the loss were part of what was lost.

REBUILDING SELF-TRUST: The Mormon church explicitly and systematically trains people not to trust themselves. "Doubt your doubts." "The natural man is an enemy to God." "Your feelings will deceive you." After years or decades of that training, leaving doesn't fix it automatically. The voice stays. What helps: starting small, noticing when you're right, building evidence for your own reliability one decision at a time.

THERAPEUTIC FRAMEWORKS THAT WORK: Internal Family Systems (IFS) — there are no bad parts. Every exiled part of yourself was trying to protect you. Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) — what actually matters to you, stripped of everything you were told to value? Narrative Therapy — re-authoring a life story that was written inside someone else's frame.

RELATIONSHIPS AND COMMUNITY: Some relationships won't survive. Not all of them. The ones built on your performance of faith are different from the ones built on who you are. Resources: Mormon Mental Health Association therapist directory, the Secular Therapy Project, the Religious Trauma Institute.

JOY AND MEANING: Not from a cosmic scoreboard. From noticing. From what you keep returning to. From what you'd defend if someone tried to take it away. From a Sunday morning with nowhere to be.

WHO THIS IS FOR: Anyone in the messy middle of post-Mormon life who wants language for what they're going through.

episodes.title: Built to Stay: How Leaving Mormonism Feels Impossible
episodes.description:
Why do intelligent, thoughtful people stay in the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints long after they've encountered information that should have sent them running? This research-dense episode answers that question through the psychology of belief — and explains why leaving is not a failure of intelligence. It explains why leaving is remarkable.

THE RESEARCH FRAMEWORK: This episode draws on a deep reading list and applies each framework specifically to Mormon belief architecture.

- Paul Harris (developmental psychology) on why children inherit belief before they can evaluate it — and why by the time questioning is possible, you're not walking into neutral territory. You're questioning the air you grew up breathing.

- Christian Smith on religion as environment before argument — Mormonism is the water, not a proposition you're presented with and invited to examine.

- Robert Cialdini on commitment and consistency — once you've made a public declaration (bearing testimony, temple covenants, mission service), your psychological need for consistency with that declaration creates enormous resistance to contradictory information.

- Leon Festinger on cognitive dissonance — the mental discomfort of holding contradictory beliefs, and the strategies the mind uses to reduce it (rationalization, selective attention, motivated skepticism toward disconfirming evidence).

- Ziva Kunda on motivated reasoning — we don't evaluate evidence neutrally; we evaluate it in the direction of the conclusion we're motivated to reach.

- Dan Kahan on identity protective cognition — when belief is identity-constitutive (as it is in Mormonism), threatening the belief is experienced as threatening the self, and the mind defends accordingly.

THE SUNK COST ARCHITECTURE: Missions, temple covenants, tithing, callings, social investment, family relationships all built inside the system. This isn't weakness. This is how commitment and investment work on human psychology. Mormonism builds those sunk costs deliberately and systematically.

THE SOCIAL COST: Leaving doesn't just mean changing your beliefs. It means risking your marriage, your family relationships, your community, your social network, and sometimes your employment. The cost of visible doubt is catastrophic and designed to be. That's why people do their questioning in private browser tabs, in the dark, at night.

WHAT THIS MEANS: If you left, you did something statistically unusual and psychologically demanding. If the people you love haven't left, this is why. This episode will not make you less frustrated. But it might make you less confused.

episodes.title: Elder James E. Christensen - The Missionary Murder the Church and the Benson Family Buried
episodes.description:
In 1977, Elder James E. Christensen — a 24-year-old with permanent neurological damage from a childhood car-train collision who desperately wanted to serve a mission for the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints — was beaten repeatedly and then scalded to death in his apartment bathtub in the Louisville, Kentucky mission by his companion, Douglas Beiger. 

Beiger admitted to the beatings. He served zero days in prison. He was given five years probation, a $3,500 fine, and sent home to his parents in Wisconsin. This is one of the cases that made Hannah angry enough to start a podcast.

THE CASE: James suffered first and second-degree burns over 40% of his body. Testimony from nurses documented belt marks on his skin. The beatings had been ongoing for months. James died of a massive brain hemorrhage on January 2nd, 1977. The defense argued his prior cranial injury caused the rupture spontaneously. Judge Michael O'Shea said "sometimes sending someone to jail is not the best thing" — and released Beiger without incarceration.

THE BENSON CONNECTION: The Louisville mission at the time was presided over by Reed Benson — son of apostle Ezra Taft Benson, future prophet. Reed Benson did not attend James's funeral. He did testify for the defense, telling the court that James had once called a girl — evidence of imperfect obedience. Pulitzer Prize-winning cartoonist Steve Benson (Reed's nephew, Ezra's grandson, who left the church publicly in 1993) documented the case in detail on the Recovery from Mormonism message board after being contacted by James's relative, Angela Voss.

THE BENSON FAMILY NARRATIVE: Steve Benson overheard family members at a General Conference gathering blaming James for his own death — saying he had been advised by his local church leaders not to serve a mission due to his disability, and that he had paid with his life for ignoring that advice.

REED SMITH'S TESTIMONY: Smith was the mission assistant who knew both Christensen and Beiger. He posted publicly about the case and concluded: "Much more accountability rests with the church, which was entirely responsible for creating the circumstances of this death, including the false and dangerous mental and emotional states of the missionaries, church leaders, and others involved." He added: "The most blame goes to Mormonism itself — the false cultish system that propagates these pressures while falsely claiming that they came from and are validated by God."

ANGELA VOSS: James's cousin's daughter, who found the case mentioned online and wrote to Steve Benson with family details that had been kept quiet for decades — and gave permission for her name to be used.This episode contains primary source documentation including court records and newspaper archives, available in full on Patreon.

episodes.title: Why Your Mormon Family Can't Hear You — Three Institutional Mechanisms That Make Listening Impossible
episodes.description:
When Jess and her sisters try to talk to their father about why they left the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, he bears his testimony at them, quotes scripture, and tells them they don't realize they're unhappy. He knows them. He loves them. And he literally cannot hear them. For years, Jess thought this was personal. Then Noah Kahan released "The Great Divide" — and thousands of ex-Mormon sons and daughters said: that's my parent.

This episode examines the three institutional mechanisms that make listening impossible — not because believing members don't care, but because the church built systems that prevent it.

MECHANISM 1: OBEDIENCE EQUALS MORALITY. When harm can't come from following the rules, the person reporting harm becomes the problem before they finish the sentence. Russell Nelson called doubters "lazy learners and lax disciples" in 2021 General Conference. Boyd K. Packer framed doubt as dangerous to spiritual health. The framework answers every question before it gets asked.

MECHANISM 2: THE PERFORMANCE OF CERTAINTY. From age three at Fast and Testimony Meeting, members are trained to say "I know" — not "I believe," not "I hope." Dallin H. Oaks literally taught that testimony is "better gained on the feet bearing it than on the knees praying for it." The Supreme Court in West Virginia v. Barnette (1943) recognized that compelled affirmation is indoctrination. Mormon culture does exactly this to children, weekly, for decades.

MECHANISM 3: THE ELIMINATION OF VISIBLE DISSENT. The September Six in 1993, Sam Young in 2018, Dallin Oaks at BYU just weeks ago — thirty-plus years of the same pattern. Sam Young collected 800 testimonies of harm from bishop interviews with children, gathered 55,000 petition signatures, went on a 23-day hunger strike — and the church made policy changes AND excommunicated him. Because the issue was never the policy. It was that he showed people dissent could work.

Jess and Hannah also break down why the exit looks sudden from the outside — because doubt could only be processed in private, in secret browser tabs, in the dark, in your body. What breaks first isn't belief. It's your nervous system.If you've been told to "move on," been told you "just want to sin," or had someone bear their testimony louder at you instead of listening — this episode is for you.

WHO THIS IS FOR: People navigating relationships across the belief divide. People whose questioning family members can't hear them. People who left and still can't explain why their family won't engage.
