episodes.title: The Psychiatrist & The Prophet: Dr. Jess Groesbeck and The Ant Hill Kids
episodes.description:
This bonus solo episode from Hannah follows directly from the Rodney Lundberg case. In 1985, Dr. C. Jess Grosbeck — the clinical director of Utah State Hospital — testified that Rodney Lundberg, who had stabbed his 11-month-old son as a test of faith, had recovered from his mental illness and was no longer a threat. Lundberg was released after three years and one month. But that's only the beginning of the story about Dr. Grosbeck.

DR. GROSBECK'S BACKGROUND: Active member of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints with missionary service. Board certified in psychiatry, forensic psychiatry, and psychoanalysis. Medical director of Utah State Hospital. Regular contributor to Sunstone and Dialogue — the more intellectually adventurous corners of Mormon scholarship. Published work on Jungian analysis, shamanism, polygamy, Joseph Smith's religious visions, and "the archetypal image of the wounded healer." His obituary describes him as "a complex man who lived his life with zeal."

THE CRITICAL QUESTION: When the person evaluating whether a man's religious delusions have been treated shares the same religious framework as the patient — can they effectively distinguish between dangerous theological breakdown and sincere but misapplied faith? If you believe in priesthood healing power, personal revelation, and the literal truth of the Abraham and Isaac story, how do you treat a man whose crime was taking those beliefs to their logical extreme?

THE ANT HILL KIDS CONNECTION: In the mid-1980s, Grosbeck read Roch Thériault's self-published autobiography. Thériault — who called himself Moses — had gathered followers in rural Ontario and Quebec, practiced polygamy with eight wives, and ran a commune the Canadian courts would eventually dismantle for systematic abuse. Grosbeck saw a living example of the themes he'd spent years studying: prophetic authority, plural marriage, wilderness living, healing rituals.What followed was a four-day compound visit, a plan to write a book together, and a deeply strange meeting in which Thériault described a dream — involving his murdered wife — that Grosbeck interpreted through Jungian symbolism.
 
Thériault left the meeting convinced that he was spiritually pregnant with the woman he had killed in an amateur surgery.Thériault was eventually convicted of murder and died in a Canadian prison in 2011.This episode asks a question that applies far beyond this one case: what happens when mental health systems share the theological framework of the patients they're meant to hold accountable?

LISTEN TO THE RODNEY LUNDBERG EPISODE FIRST — this episode builds directly on it.

episodes.title: Mormon Women Are Filling Up Our Screens (and the New York Times is here for it)
episodes.description:
The New York Times published a trend piece asking why there are so many Mormons on reality TV. A podcast host named Rebbe Brasfield was quoted saying the best export of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints is "our women." The Times printed it without follow-up.

This episode is the follow-up the Times should have done.

THE EXPORT PROBLEM: An export is something you produce, brand, and send out for someone else's consumption. It is not a person with agency. When a woman can use that language about herself and other Mormon women — approvingly, to a national newspaper — she has internalized her own commodification so thoroughly that she can say it out loud without hearing what it reveals. The extraction model that treats women's labor and image as institutional capital is not new. What changed is the distribution platform.

THE HEATHER GAY SMOKING GUN: Heather Gay, cast member of The Real Housewives of Salt Lake City, is quoted in the article saying: "We've been trained our whole lives to spin it positive." And then she notes that the conditioning that taught Mormon women to perform perfection in their real lives is exactly what makes them naturals at performing for social media. She said the thing. And the Times just nodded and moved on.

WHAT ELEVATED STANDARDS ACTUALLY MEANS: The article quotes someone praising Mormon women for their "elevated standards" as employees. What elevated standards actually means: you've been trained from childhood to perform perfection, to never show weakness, to manage everyone's perception of you at all times. That looks like a great employee. It is also a trauma response. It is what happens when your worthiness has been evaluated since you were three years old.

THE COMPETITION THAT CAN'T BE NAMED: Cast member Miranda Hope says there's "a little bit of an undertone of competition" in Mormon culture. The ocean is a little bit damp. Salt Lake City has the second-highest density of plastic surgeons in the country — ahead of Los Angeles, behind only Miami. Utah's rates of antidepressants, eating disorders, and anxiety disorders are among the highest in the country. And one woman commented on a Mormon Stories episode: "I am a 54-year-old Mormon woman and I have had plastic surgery due to unspoken competition towards other women in the church. I can't believe I said that out loud."

THE CHURCH'S ACTUAL POSITION: When asked to comment on the shows, the Church declined and referred to a statement saying the productions depict "life sales and practices blatantly inconsistent with the teachings of the church." So the Times frames these shows as evidence of Mormon openness and modern Mormon women — and the church says, no, these women don't represent us.

THE QUESTION THE TIMES DIDN'T ASK: Why are we celebrating that some women have figured out how to monetize their own oppression — instead of asking why that oppression exists in the first place?

WHO THIS IS FOR: Anyone who watches these shows and wants a critical framework for what they're actually seeing.

episodes.title: A Mormon Father Stabbed His 11-Month Old As a Test of Faith. His "Treatment" Lasted 3 Years. Then He Came Home
episodes.description:
On November 23rd, 1981, in Logan, Utah — the heart of Cache Valley, the center of Mormon country — 26-year-old Rodney Lundberg placed his 11-month-old son Justin on a table, gripped a butcher knife with both hands, closed his eyes, and slowly lowered the blade toward his son. He was waiting for God to intervene. The way God intervened for Abraham.God didn't stop him. Justin bled to death over the next two hours while his father, his mother, and their devoutly LDS neighbors performed priesthood blessings and attempted to cast out demons — and nobody called 911.

THIS IS NOT A STORY ABOUT ONE MAN'S MENTAL BREAKDOWN. It's a story about how an entire religious culture enabled, validated, and ultimately protected a child killer — and what that reveals about the doctrine of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.

THE THEOLOGY THAT MADE THIS POSSIBLE: The Abraham and Isaac narrative is not fringe theology in Mormonism. Official LDS teaching materials ask students to consider "what would be the one hardest thing for you to let go of — would you do it?" The official church handbook states that God's test of Abraham was designed "to enable him to attain unto knowledge he could not obtain in any other way." When Rodney raised that knife, he was doing exactly what he had been taught: demonstrating the highest form of faith, trusting that divine intervention would come, obeying God completely.

THE PRIESTHOOD HEALING DOCTRINE: Doctrine and Covenants 42:48 states that the sick "shall be healed" through the laying on of hands with sufficient faith. Current LDS prophet Dallin H. Oaks has taught that faith in priesthood healing power "cannot produce a result contrary to the will of him whose priesthood it is." For two hours, this doctrine was the reason four adults didn't call an ambulance.

THE INSANITY PLEA AND WHAT IT AVOIDED: Dr. Ronald Markman — the nationally prominent forensic psychiatrist who evaluated the Manson family and the Hillside Strangler — diagnosed Rodney with "paranoia, identity confusion, and grandiosity." Hannah unpacks why these diagnoses describe Mormon priesthood culture more than they describe mental illness. The insanity plea was accepted. No jury ever heard testimony about LDS doctrine. No prosecutor ever asked a church leader: "Do you teach that God speaks directly to faithful members? Do you teach that priesthood holders can raise the dead?"

DR. C. JESS GROSBECK AND THE CONFLICT OF INTEREST: The Utah State Hospital clinical director who declared Rodney cured and safe for release after 3 years and 1 month was himself an active LDS member with missionary service, deep interest in polygamy and Jungian analysis, and a documented fascination with prophetic authority. The episode explores what it means when a man evaluating dangerous religious belief shares the same theological framework as his patient.

LEANNE LUNDBERG: Married to Rodney for 28 years after the murder of her first child. Temple sealed. Obstruction of justice charges filed and then disappeared from the docket. No public record of child protective services involvement with the second son — born while Rodney was institutionalized.

BRENT ALLEN: The neighbor who watched Justin bleed to death and used his priesthood to cast out demons while Rodney tried to raise his son. He went on to teach Mormon youth in seminary. 

Full primary source documentation — court records, newspaper archives, preliminary hearing testimony — available on Patreon.

episodes.title: Mormon Control Mechanisms — Unpaid Labor, Endless Meetings, Surveillance with a Casserole, and the Treadmill That Never Stops
episodes.description:
How does one religion manage to consume so much of its members' time, money, mental energy, and social life? This episode answers that question systematically. The Mormon church is what sociologists call a totalizing institution — it regulates behavior, beliefs, social circles, identity, and life structure. This didn't happen by accident. Every piece of it evolved over 200 years.

THE CALLING SYSTEM: Members are assigned — not asked — to volunteer positions called callings. You don't say no to a calling. The Bishop didn't call you. God called you. If you're working full time raising five children and they ask you to teach early morning seminary at 6am, you cannot say "that doesn't work for me" without telling Heavenly Father to get lost. Joseph F. Smith taught that refusing callings was a sign of spiritual weakness and compared it to resisting the Holy Ghost.The church has a $200 billion investment portfolio. They cannot afford to pay people to clean the bathrooms or teach Sunday school. Members provide all teaching, administration, maintenance, and service that keeps congregations functioning — for free, framed as divine assignment. By 1930, the church had fully eliminated paid local clergy and shifted all that labor to members while centralizing financial resources in Salt Lake City.

THE MEETING SCHEDULE: The three-hour Sunday block (now two hours since 2019) replaced an earlier model of meetings scattered across the week — Primary on Tuesday, youth on Wednesday, Relief Society on Thursday. The consolidation was supposed to reduce burden. Instead, the church quietly expanded behind-the-scenes meeting expectations. The reduction in 2019 was the church taking credit for returning time it had originally taken. Don't be fooled: leadership meetings, temple attendance, lesson preparation, and ministering assignments still consume entire days.

THE DISCIPLINARY COUNCIL (NOW CALLED MEMBERSHIP COUNCIL): A formal ecclesiastical court — run by lay clergy with no legal training — that can determine whether you're excommunicated (now "withdrawn membership") for adultery, apostasy, criticizing church leaders, or LGBTQ relationships. The accused is not given a transcript. There is no right to representation. Decisions are considered divine revelation. Men who commit domestic violence are "counseled" and remain in good standing. Women who commit adultery are dragged before a panel of men to describe their sexual behavior in detail.

SURVEILLANCE WITH A CASSEROLE: The ministering program — formerly visiting teaching and home teaching — assigns members to regularly visit other members, check on their welfare, and report to leadership. Monthly compliance was tracked and reported up the chain as KPIs. In 1980, the church bragged in General Conference that home teaching compliance had hit 75%. The phrase "surveillance with a casserole" describes the system perfectly.

INFORMATION CONTROL: The Correlation Program, launched in the 1960s, centralized all church materials, curriculum, artwork, and historical messaging under one committee of male general authorities. Relief Society's independent magazine — published for nearly a century — was eliminated. Any scholarship, journalism, or survivor testimony that contradicts church narratives is labeled "anti-Mormon." Metallica was anti-Mormon. No Man Knows My History was anti-Mormon. Peer-reviewed academic research is anti-Mormon. The church invented fake news.

THE PERFECTIONISM TREADMILL: You can never do enough. Scripture study? Do more. Family Home Evening? Better lesson. Temple attendance? More frequently. The system ensures members are always striving and never arriving. Utah leads the nation in antidepressant use. It also has one of the highest youth suicide rates in the country. The connection is not subtle.Jess has been out of the church for over three decades. She still catches herself asking "what's the right thing to do?" instead of "what do I want?" The programming runs deep.

episodes.title: Mormon Gender Roles — Motherhood as Divine Destiny, Priesthood for 11-Year-Old Boys, and the Eternal Subordination of Women
episodes.description:
In Mormon theology, gender is eternal. It existed before birth and continues after death. Women were foreordained in the pre-mortal existence to be mothers. Men hold exclusive access to priesthood authority and church leadership. And an 11-year-old boy who just figured out how to use deodorant has more religious authority than your grandmother who spent 70 years serving the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.This episode covers the theology, the history, and real-world impact of Mormon gender roles.

THE MOTHERHOOD DOCTRINE: The church teaches that motherhood is women's highest calling — not just preferred, but divinely mandated. This emphasis intensified in the 1950s and 60s when leaders like David O. McKay and Ezra Taft Benson explicitly taught that mothers working outside the home was against God's plan. The 1995 Proclamation on the Family — released during the same-sex marriage debates in Hawaii — codified these roles as eternal doctrine. Working mothers in Relief Society lessons often faced pointed comments about women who "think they're so clever with their cars and their careers." 

THE PRIESTHOOD: In the Mormon church, the male-only priesthood isn't ceremonial. It's practical power. Only priesthood holders can perform baptisms, confirmations, temple ceremonies, blessings, and administrations to the sick. Only men can serve as bishops, stake presidents, general authorities, or prophets. A 12-year-old boy deacon passing the sacrament outranks every woman in the building, including the Relief Society president with 40 years of church service. 

Joseph Smith told the Relief Society in 1842 that he was "turning the key" to them. Women gave blessings to their families regularly through the 1800s. By 1946, the church explicitly told women to call the elders instead. 

THE MODESTY BLANKET: In Hannah's niece's Young Women's group, teenage girls whose skirts were deemed too short by female leaders were made to sit through the lesson with a blanket over their laps — the modesty blanket. Jess spent her entire youth being told that her shoulders were spiritually dangerous to righteous priesthood holders. 

RELIEF SOCIETY: Founded in 1842 as a semi-autonomous women's organization with its own finances, publication, and independent programs. In 1971, all Relief Society activities were correlated under priesthood direction. The organization lost its budget, its magazine, and its autonomy. Today, women run the organization's programming but require a bishop's approval for their activities. Grown women with more education and experience than their bishop ask a man if they can have a bake sale.

POLYGAMY'S ONGOING LEGACY: The church officially ended polygamy in 1890 but still practices it theologically — men can be sealed to multiple women in the temple after a first wife's death; women cannot be sealed to multiple men. Eternal polygamy is current LDS doctrine. Hannah's mother, who has borderline personality disorder & a profound fear of abandonment, was told she would eventually be made "okay with it" by God.

THE FINANCIAL TRAP: Women who follow the motherhood doctrine — prioritizing family over education and career — often find themselves with no financial resources, no work experience, & no credential if they need to leave an unhealthy or abusive relationship.

episodes.title: What Mormons Actually Believe - Golden Plates, Magic Stones, Becoming Gods, and the One True Church
episodes.description:
This is the first episode in our Foundations series for Never-Mormons. If someone in your life is Mormon — or post-Mormon — and you're not sure what that actually means, this is where to start.

JOSEPH SMITH'S ORIGIN STORY: In 1820 upstate New York, 14-year-old Joseph Smith claimed that God the Father and Jesus Christ personally appeared to him in a grove of trees — during the height of the Second Great Awakening — and told him all existing churches were wrong. Three years later, an angel named Moroni directed him to golden plates buried in a nearby hill. Joseph translated those plates — not by looking at them, but by putting a seer stone into a hat and dictating what he saw in the darkness — producing the 531 pages of the Book of Mormon.

THE BOOK OF MORMON'S PROBLEMS: It addresses 19th century theological debates that wouldn't exist for another thousand years. It quotes extensively from the King James Bible — including translation errors — even though the KJV wasn't published until 1769. It describes horses, cattle, steel, and wheat in pre-Columbian America where none of those existed. DNA evidence has found zero Middle Eastern ancestry among Native Americans, despite the Book's claim that they're descended from Israelites. The church quietly changed "principal ancestors" to "among the ancestors" when genetics became inconvenient.

THE BOOK OF ABRAHAM: Joseph purchased Egyptian papyri from a traveling exhibition and declared they contained the writings of the biblical Abraham. When the papyri were discovered in the Metropolitan Museum of Art in the 1960s (thought lost in the Chicago fire), Egyptologists identified them as a common funeral text for a man named Hor — with no trace of Abraham anywhere. The church's current explanation: Joseph was seeing with "spiritual eyes." The papyrus was just a catalyst. Invisible ink.

POLYGAMY: Joseph Smith began marrying additional wives in 1833 — approximately 30 to 40 of them — while publicly denying the practice. His youngest wife, Helen Mar Kimball, was 14. He told her and others that the salvation of their entire families depended on their compliance. Months before his death, he preached publicly that he could only find one wife. He was practicing with at least 30 at the time.

THREE DEGREES OF GLORY: Joseph Smith borrowed the concept of a three-tiered heaven from Emmanuel Swedenborg, a Swedish philosopher who published Heaven and Hell in 1758. Miraculously, 74 years later Joseph received a revelation about the same three-tiered system. The Celestial Kingdom is for faithful temple Mormons who become gods and rule their own planets. The Terrestrial Kingdom is for the almost-Mormon-enough. The Telestial Kingdom is for serious sinners — but Joseph Smith said it was so great that if you could see it, you'd die just to get there.

THE SECOND ANOINTING: The highest and most secret Mormon temple ordinance, in which a couple's exaltation is guaranteed and sealed. Hannah suspects her parents have received it. The implication: even Hannah's apostasy may not be enough to keep her out of the Celestial Kingdom. Her parents' coattails reach into eternity whether she wants them to or not.

TITHING AND THE ONLY TRUE CHURCH: 10% of your gross income, required for temple access, tracked through annual tithing settlement. And Mormons don't just believe their church is good — they believe it is the only true church on earth, with the only valid priesthood authority. Every other Christian denomination has been in apostasy for 1,700 years. Joseph Smith restored everything.

episodes.title: The Word of Wisdom — Why Mormons Won't Drink Coffee But Will Drink Five Red Bulls Before Noon
episodes.description:
If you've ever wondered why your Mormon coworker drinks Red Bull but won't touch coffee, or why there are dirty soda shops across Utah with cult followings among devout members, or why Mitt Romney's Diet Coke order became a doctrinal crisis in 2012 — this episode explains all of it.

THE ORIGIN: In 1833, Joseph Smith received a revelation about health. The revelation explicitly stated it was given "not by commandment or constraint." It advised against alcohol, tobacco, hot drinks, and excessive meat consumption. At the time, the Church's own stores in Nauvoo sold both coffee and tea. Joseph Smith preached the Word of Wisdom — and then smoked cigars afterward. He drank wine in Carthage Jail the night before his death.

HOW IT BECAME MANDATORY: For the first 70 years, the Word of Wisdom was loose guidance, not a requirement. It wasn't until President Heber J. Grant — in the early 1900s, during national Prohibition — decided to make strict abstinence from alcohol, coffee, and tea a requirement for temple access. What began as friendly advice became grounds for spiritual exclusion. Conveniently aligned with the temperance movement. Definitely revelation.

THE CAFFEINE CONTRADICTION: The prohibition is against coffee and tea specifically — not caffeine. This means Members can drink Diet Coke, Mountain Dew, Red Bull, Monster, and pre-workout powder while considering a cup of green tea a serious sin. Utah has one of the highest per capita Diet Coke consumption rates in the United States. The state also has one of the highest antidepressant prescription rates. These data points are not unrelated.

THE DIET COKE LOOPHOLE: BYU did not sell caffeinated beverages for decades. Then Mitt Romney was photographed with a regular Diet Coke in 2012 and confirmed he drank it. BYU shortly thereafter began selling caffeinated beverages on campus. Heavenly Father's revelation on caffeine appears to have been updated by a presidential campaign photo.

THE DIRTY SODA PHENOMENON: Unique to Mormon culture: a large Diet Coke with cream, coconut syrup, and lime juice. More calories than a Big Mac. No coffee beans. Temple-worthy. The dirty soda shop is a Utah institution that would be baffling to literally anyone outside the culture.

THE MEAT THING: The Word of Wisdom also teaches that meat should be eaten "sparingly." This section receives approximately zero enforcement. Utah is beef country. The average Fast Sunday potluck has seventeen meat dishes. The meat provisions are treated the way most people treat speed limits — more of a general vibe.

FOOD STORAGE AND PREPPING: Mormon preparedness culture — stockpiling a year's supply of food, water, and emergency materials — flows directly from the Word of Wisdom's ethos of self-sufficiency. Many faithful Mormon homes have a dedicated storage room with 50-pound bags of wheat, cases of freeze-dried food, and a hand-powered grain grinder (because you won't have electricity in the apocalypse). As Jess points out: the grain is useless without the grinder. Her family had 600 pounds of wheat berries and no way to eat them.

SOCIAL SURVEILLANCE: Your beverage choice is a public declaration of your temple worthiness. Members who see you with a Starbucks cup know immediately. Hannah's mother would not drink non-alcoholic beer because people might think she was drinking real beer — even if she wasn't. The performance of abstinence is as important as the abstinence itself. Avoiding the appearance of evil is doctrine.

episodes.title: Nice But Not Kind: The Everyday Harm of Growing Up in a High-Control Religion
episodes.description:
The first time Jess heard Dr. Gina Colvin's concept of "ordinary Mormon trauma," she said it felt like someone had reached into her childhood and perfectly named experiences she didn't even know were traumatic. Not the dramatic incidents. The everyday ones.This isn't an episode about obvious abuse or clear dysfunction. It's about the normalized, invisible harm of growing up in a culture that teaches you to be nice but not kind, obedient but not teachable, disciplined but not regulated.

DR. COLVIN'S FRAMEWORK: Gina Colvin is a former Mormon in New Zealand, host of the podcast A Thoughtful Faith. She identified that ordinary Mormon trauma is "that everyday experience of being in a culture that has never resolved its formative pathologies or repented of its institutional sins." The trauma gets passed down. Mormon parents who experienced it raise their children inside the same system — perpetuating the cycle without recognizing what they're doing.

THE CONTRASTS THAT EXPLAIN EVERYTHING: Nice, but not kind. Disciplined, but not regulated. Curious, but not open. Service-oriented, but not care-oriented. Devout, but not holy. Worthy, but not good. Confident, but not integrated. Obedient, but not teachable. Dependable, but not safe. Each of these hits differently when you sit with it.

THE THEOLOGICAL CONTRADICTION: The Book of Mormon teaches that mankind should "act for themselves and not be acted upon." Agency is supposedly the whole point of existence. And yet the church implements the opposite — callings issued without consent, policies changed overnight, members always in a reactive state, never in control.

PERSONAL STORIES: Jess's experience of growing up knowing her ambitions didn't align with what the church said her divine role was. Hannah's experience of being raised in a family where her father worked for the Church Educational System — allowed to ask doctrinal questions, but only within acceptable parameters, and never with satisfying answers. Both women trace how the "death by a thousand cuts" of daily self-betrayal created adults who struggled to trust their own instincts.

THE BODY KEEPS SCORE: How dissociation from your own inner life — your feelings, your instincts, your gut signals — is one of the most lasting effects of this environment.

WHO THIS IS FOR: People who say "I had a fine Mormon childhood" but can't figure out why they ended up in therapy unpacking why they can't trust themselves. People who are still trying to name what happened to them.

We explore Mormon cultural weirdness, doctrinal whiplash, religious trauma, Mormon true crime, and the messy, glorious road to recovery. episodes.title: Mark Hofmann: The Mormon Forger Who Fooled Prophets and Killed Two People
episodes.description:
The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints paid nearly $350,000 — roughly $900,000 in today's dollars — to suppress historical documents that were later revealed to be complete forgeries. The man who sold them was a returned missionary, temple-recommend holder, Eagle Scout, and eventual murderer.

Mark Hofmann didn't just forge documents. He forged the perfect Mormon resume, and the LDS Church bought both.

In this episode, Jess and Hannah walk through one of the most bizarre and consequential scandals in Mormon history: how a master forger with an encyclopedic knowledge of early church history spent five years selling fake documents to the highest levels of LDS leadership — then built pipe bombs when the debts came due.

What we cover:
— The Anthon Transcript forgery — Hofmann's 1980 entry point into LDS document dealing, exploiting Mormon obsession with proving the historicity of the Book of Mormon. The Anthon Transcript is the supposed record of characters copied from Joseph Smith's golden plates, taken to Columbia professor Charles Anthon for authentication.

— The Salamander Letter — Hofmann's most notorious forgery: a letter suggesting Joseph Smith was visited not by the Angel Moroni, but by a shape-shifting white salamander. Why did senior church leaders take it seriously? Because early Mormonism was soaked in folk magic — seer stones, treasure digging, divining rods — and the salamander wasn't actually that far off from documented history.

— The Joseph Smith III Blessing — a forged document suggesting Joseph Smith designated his son, not Brigham Young, as his successor. This directly threatened the legitimacy of the Utah-based church and supported RLDS claims to the original movement.

— The Lucy Mack Smith letters and the 1825 Joseph Smith letter — forgeries depicting Joseph as a money digger and folk magician, contradicting the sanitized prophetic narrative the institutional church had spent generations building.

— Why church leaders bought them without expert verification — Hofmann's temple recommend and missionary record functioned as an invisibility cloak. The Mormon worthiness system, designed to assess character, became the mechanism for bypassing skepticism entirely.

— The October 1985 bombings — the death of document collector Steven Christensen and Kathy Sheets, wife of his former business partner, and how a third bomb detonated in Hofmann's own car. Whether it was a botched suicide attempt or a calculated misdirection, it ended his operation.

— The church's damage control — Elder Dallin Oaks gave a speech at BYU defending the Salamander Letter before it was exposed as a fake, attempting to reconcile a shape-shifting amphibian with Mormon theology. When the forgeries collapsed, the church tried to minimize its relationship with Hofmann despite years of private first-presidency meetings.

— What this exposed about the LDS Church's relationship with its own history — not that they were conned, but that they were buying documents specifically to bury them. The institutional fear wasn't of forgery. It was of their own historical record.

The Hofmann case is inseparable from a larger pattern: the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints is built on specific historical truth claims, which means its leaders have consistently treated inconvenient history as an institutional threat to be managed rather than a truth to be reckoned with. Hofmann understood this better than almost anyone. That's why it worked.
If a magical salamander letter almost unraveled a global religion, the foundation was always shakier than the testimony meeting suggested.

episodes.title: Early Tracks: Abducted in Plain Sight
episodes.description:
Robert Berchtold kidnapped Jan Broberg twice. He was a neighbor, a returned missionary, a trusted family friend — and the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints called what he did an "emotional problem."

This episode breaks down the Netflix documentary Abducted in Plain Sight through the lens of Mormon institutional culture. Not to mock the Broberg parents — but to ask the harder question: what belief system made this possible?

WHAT THIS EPISODE COVERS:

THE GROOMING ARCHITECTURE: Berchtold didn't just groom Jan. He groomed Bob and Mary Ann Broberg separately — seducing both parents into sexual relationships to create leverage, complicity, and silence. His access to their daughter was purchased through their shame.

THE ALIEN NARRATIVE: Berchtold used a tape recorder with fake alien voices to convince Jan she had a cosmic mission to bear his child — or her family would die. This wasn't random. He chose this narrative specifically because Jan had grown up in a faith tradition that taught her she was foreordained, spiritually exceptional, and cosmically chosen. He used Mormon theology as a grooming tool.

THE INSTITUTIONAL SHIELD: During this period, the LDS Church had a policy requiring abuse allegations be reported to Church headquarters before contacting law enforcement. Bishops were instructed to call the Church's law firm first. Berchtold had been caught molesting other girls before the Broberg kidnappings. Nothing was reported to police.

THE FIRST ABDUCTION: October 1974. Berchtold drugged Jan with Xanax he had been stockpiling specifically for this purpose, staged a crime scene, and drove her to Mexico where he had married her under Mexican law. She was 12. The Brobergs waited days before reporting her missing. Direct quote from the documentary: "We didn't want to ruin the friendship. We didn't want to create a scandal."

THE SECOND ABDUCTION: 1976. The charges from the first kidnapping had been dropped after the Brobergs were pressured — by the Church and by Berchtold's attorney — to sign affidavits calling it a misunderstanding. Jan disappeared again for over 90 days. She was eventually located at a Catholic girls' school in Pasadena, where Berchtold had enrolled her under a fake name and drove over on weekends from Salt Lake City.

THE LEGAL EPILOGUE: Berchtold evaded serious consequences for decades. Six additional victims came forward after the publication of Mary Ann Broberg's memoir. He was convicted of raping one of them, served one year in jail, and died by suicide in 2005 — the day before he was to be sentenced on additional charges.

THE REAL QUESTION: The Brobergs were not uniquely stupid. They were trained by a high-control institution to obey authority, avoid scandal, suppress doubt, forgive unconditionally, and trust male priesthood holders by default. Berchtold didn't just exploit a family. He exploited a system — and the system was designed to be exploited.

WHO THIS EPISODE IS FOR: Anyone who watched the Netflix documentary and wanted more analysis. Anyone trying to understand how Mormon institutional culture enables abuse. Anyone who grew up in a high-control religious environment and is still untangling why it was so hard to trust your own instincts.

If you grew up in this system and you're wondering why it's hard to speak up, trust your gut, or set boundaries — you were not broken. You were trained not to.

episodes.title: The Mormon Patriarchal Blessing — A Personal Prophecy About Your Eternal Future, Your Tribe of Israel, and What Happens When It Doesn't Come True
episodes.description:
When Mormon teenagers reach a certain age, they sit with an elderly man who places his hands on their head and delivers a personal prophecy about their eternal future — their tribal lineage from ancient Israel, spiritual gifts, life calling, and the blessings available to them if they remain righteous. This is called a patriarchal blessing.Almost everyone gets assigned to the tribe of Ephraim. Even the Korean convert. Even the Swedish immigrant. Even if your DNA says otherwise.

WHAT IT IS: The patriarchal blessing is a form of personal revelation delivered by a church-appointed patriarch — typically an older man considered spiritually gifted. The blessing is transcribed word for word and given to the recipient as a personal document. Members are encouraged to study it throughout their lives, return to it in times of difficulty, and treat it as guidance from God specific to them.

THE TRIBAL LINEAGE DOCTRINE: Mormon theology teaches that every member is literally descended from one of the twelve tribes of ancient Israel — not metaphorically adopted in, but genetically descended, with their bloodline literally rewritten at baptism. The patriarch declares which tribe you belong to. It is almost always Ephraim. Occasionally Manasseh for converts of Pacific Islander or Latin American heritage. Jessica can't remember her tribe. She's pretty sure it was Rachel or Sarah (those are names, not tribes — illustrating the general haziness of the experience for many recipients).

THE CAVEAT THAT MAKES IT UNFALSIFIABLE: Every blessing comes with a conditional clause. These blessings are available to you if you remain faithful and endure to the end. So if the promises don't materialize — if the mission predicted never happens, if the spouse described never appears, if the children promised never arrive — the fault is never the prophecy. It's your faithfulness. The blessing is always right. You are always the variable.

THE PRESSURE: Members often agonize over their patriarchal blessings well into adulthood — parsing phrases, looking for guidance, wondering what it means that theirs mentioned leadership when they feel invisible, or children when they can't conceive, or specific callings that never came. Some members feel their blessing has been fulfilled. Others carry the weight of unfulfilled prophecy as evidence of their own spiritual failure.

THE MURDER CASE: In one of the most disturbing true crime cases in Jess and Hannah's research, a patriarchal blessing plays a central role. A man's blessing told him he would be a good husband to his wives — plural. He interpreted this as divine permission for a second wife. His first wife's fate became a true crime story. This case gets its own full episode.

WHY THIS MATTERS FOR NEVER-MORMONS: If a Mormon in your life seems unusually attached to a particular outcome — a mission, a specific kind of marriage, a life path that seems rigid even when circumstances change — their patriarchal blessing may be the reason. It is a document they have been told is God's personal message to them. Questioning it can feel like questioning God.

WHAT JESS AND HANNAH MAKE OF IT: Both received patriarchal blessings. Neither remembers them in any detail. Both agree that the specificity of the promises varies wildly by patriarch. Hannah's lingering question: why does God need to run a rotating name database organized by date, when the whole point is personal divine communication?

episodes.title: What Mormon Missions Are Actually Like - The Companion System, Door Knocking, Obedience Culture, and What Happens When It Goes Wrong
episodes.description:
Between the ages of 18 and 25, hundreds of thousands of young Mormons leave home for 18 to 24 months to knock on strangers' doors and tell them about the gospel of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. They go two by two. They are never alone. They cannot date, cannot call home except on designated days, cannot watch television, cannot listen to non-church music, and work approximately 16-hour days in an environment that controls nearly everything about their daily lives.This is the Mormon mission. And for many Latter-day Saints, it is the defining experience of their young adulthood.

THE COMPANION SYSTEM: Missionaries are paired with a companion and are never — under any circumstances — supposed to be alone with them or separated from them. The companion system is framed as spiritual protection and accountability. In practice, it means you grieve in front of someone. You process everything with someone present. You have no privacy, no space, and no relief from the person assigned to you — regardless of whether you get along. The case of Elder James E. Christensen (the subject of a full episode) demonstrates what this system looks like when it goes catastrophically wrong.

THE CALL: Missions are assigned by church headquarters, not chosen. Members submit an application and receive their assignment in a letter from the prophet. Opening the call letter — in front of family, often filmed — is a major cultural milestone. You might be sent anywhere in the world. You are expected to accept with gratitude and faith.

THE CULTURE OF OBEDIENCE: Missionaries are ranked. Mission presidents hold enormous authority over 18 and 19-year-olds. Obedience is framed as the first law of heaven. Missionaries who struggle — with faith, with mental health, with the relentlessness of rejection — are often told their struggles reflect insufficient faith. Going home early carries significant shame.

THE DEAR JOHN LETTER: So common it has an official name. The letter from the person back home who has moved on. Jess was on the other side of one of these — she simply stopped writing to Josh, who was thousands of miles away in a controlled environment with no way to process the loss. She reflects on this with genuine remorse. They were both 19. They were both inside a system that prioritized the mission over human connection.

FLIRT TO CONVERT: The documented missionary strategy of showing romantic interest in potential converts. A missionary letter found online explicitly describes this as "a very effective way to introduce someone to the gospel." The person being pursued often has no idea the interest was strategic until the conversion attempt begins. If they don't convert, the relationship typically evaporates. If they do, they've joined a high-control religion for love rather than belief.

WHEN IT GOES WRONG: Mission culture creates conditions where abuse can occur and be hidden. Young people in a hierarchical system, isolated from family, trained in obedience to authority, with no private space — the vulnerabilities are structural. The case of Elder James E. Christensen shows what it looks like when those vulnerabilities are catastrophic. His killer served zero prison time. His mission president testified for the defense.

THE RETURN: Coming home from a mission is its own disorientation. You've spent 18-24 months as a full-time representative of the church. Now you're supposed to go to college, date, get married, start a career — all within the framework of a faith you may have privately begun to question while you were still required to teach it door to door.

episodes.title: Mormon Folklore - Three Nephites, Cain as Bigfoot, Garments that Stop Bullets, and Stories that Circulate Alongside Official Doctrine
episodes.description:
Every religion has its official doctrine and its folk religion — the stories that circulate in wards and at family dinners, whispered in Sunday school and told with full conviction at Fast and Testimony Meeting, that have no official doctrinal status but are as culturally powerful as any canonized teaching.Mormon folklore is particularly rich. This episode covers the most significant examples — what they are, where they come from, and what they reveal about Mormon culture.

THE THREE NEPHITES: In the Book of Mormon, three disciples of Christ are granted immortality and wander the earth doing good until the Second Coming. In Mormon folk culture, these men are everywhere — rescuing stranded motorists, appearing at bedsides of the dying, and then vanishing before they can be properly thanked. The stories follow a consistent structure: a stranger appears at a moment of crisis, provides exactly the help needed, and disappears. Only later does the witness realize the stranger must have been one of the Three Nephites. There is no official church position verifying any of these accounts. The accounts are constant.

CAIN AS BIGFOOT: In 1835, church leader David W. Patten reported encountering a very tall, hairy man with no hair on his body and dark skin who identified himself as Cain — the biblical fratricide condemned by God to wander the earth as a fugitive and vagabond. Multiple Mormon accounts of Bigfoot sightings have been filtered through this theological lens: Cain was cursed to walk the earth, he cannot die, and occasional human encounters with large hairy creatures are therefore encounters with the world's first murderer. This is not official doctrine. It is very much unofficial belief in certain Mormon communities.

GARMENT MIRACLES: The temple garment — the sacred underwear worn by endowed members — is believed by many members to provide literal physical protection. Stories circulate of garments deflecting bullets, preventing burns from fires, and protecting accident victims in ways that defy medical explanation. The garment, where it covered skin, was unaffected. Where it didn't cover, the injury was severe. These stories are told as testimony of the garment's protective power. The church neither officially endorses nor discourages these accounts.

THE TESTIMONY MEETING ECOSYSTEM: Fast and Testimony Meeting is the first Sunday of each month, when members stand and share their personal witnesses of the gospel. In practice, this meeting also functions as the primary distribution channel for Mormon folklore. Stories of miraculous intervention, priesthood healings, answered prayers, and divine guidance are shared and reshared across wards and across generations — often evolving in specificity with each retelling.

THE THIN LINE: What distinguishes Mormon folklore from Mormon doctrine is often just time. The stories that circulate now as unofficial legend were often taught as authoritative truth by earlier church leaders. Blood atonement doctrine. The curse of dark skin. Racial theories about the pre-mortal existence. These were official. They became uncomfortable. They became quietly distanced from. The folklore that persists often preserves the ideological DNA of teachings the church no longer wants to claim officially.

WHY THIS MATTERS: Understanding Mormon folklore is essential to understanding Mormon culture. These stories aren't peripheral. They shape how members understand healing, protection, divine intervention, and the role of obedience in determining outcomes. If someone's garment miracle story is the foundation of their testimony, official church position on garments is beside the point.

episodes.title: Why Mormon Bishops Ask Children About Masturbation Behind Closed Doors - And What Finally (Sort of) Changed
episodes.description: Your worthiness to enter God's house is determined by a volunteer with zero professional training asking you detailed personal questions in a private room. He might be your dentist. He has a handbook. The Mormon bishop interview system assesses members on sexual purity, tithing compliance, testimony, and sustaining church leaders — but in practice, questions about masturbation and pornography use are common, because Mormon theology frames sexual sin as second in severity only to murder. Hannah had to confess to her bishop for dry humping at age 13. Spencer W. Kimball's The Miracle of Forgiveness — which taught that masturbation could lead to homosexuality and that assault victims who didn't fight back bore some responsibility — served as the de facto bishop's manual for decades. Sam Young collected thousands of survivor testimonies, gathered 55,000 petition signatures, and went on a 23-day hunger strike to end one-on-one interviews with minors. The church excommunicated him, made policy changes, and framed them as inspired leadership. The worthiness paradox: Hannah's mother would answer yes to every recommend question honestly — and then no to the final one. In this episode: the structure and questions of a Mormon bishop worthiness interview; the theological basis for sexual sin ranking second to murder in LDS doctrine; Spencer W. Kimball's The Miracle of Forgiveness — its teachings, its reach, and its official status; the history of sexually explicit questions in bishop interviews with minors; Sam Young's Protect LDS Children campaign — the petition, the hunger strike, and the excommunication; the policy changes the church made and how they were framed; the worthiness paradox and what it means to fail a question you've never been able to honestly pass.
